sources.notes: Statement and constraints: n segments with independent probabilities p/q; compute probability every point 1..m is covered by exactly one segment.
Official editorial/code confirms factoring absent probabilities and running DP over segment endpoints.

reference_candidates.notes: Candidate checked by search/open attempt; not used as a consulted solution source.

annotation.summary: Given random segments on [1,m], find the probability that each integer point is covered by exactly one chosen segment.
annotation.constraints: n,m up to contest-sized linear limits; modular arithmetic under 998244353; an O(n+m) endpoint DP is intended.
annotation.core_idea: For a valid outcome, selected segments must form an exact non-overlapping partition of [1,m]. Factor out the probability that all segments are absent, then choosing a segment [l,r] contributes odds p/(1-p). Let dp[x] be the weighted probability of exactly covering prefix [1,x]; each segment adds dp[r] += dp[l-1] * odds.
tricks: turn exact cover into interval partition DP
use odds p/(1-p) for selected segments

solution_variants.name: odds-interval-cover-dp
solution_variants.summary: Initialize dp[0] with product of all absent probabilities; process segments by right endpoint and extend exact prefix covers by multiplying the selected segment odds.

tags.evidence: The main state is the probability weight for exactly covered prefixes, and transitions multiply segment odds.
The reduction depends on independent segment probabilities and odds p/(1-p).
The exact-cover condition becomes a prefix DP over covered coordinates.
